figures.caption: Figure 1. Map of the 17 farms where samples were collected across four designated Regions 141 (see key). Each point represents one farm, with distinct shapes indicating the designated Region. 142 Farms were assigned an abbreviated region code (Guatuso = GTS; Upala = UPA; Guácimo = 143 GCM; Talamanca = TAL; Puntarenas = PUNT), followed by a number indicating the order in 144 which collections were made. 145

Figure 8. Results of Isolation by Distance (IBD) analyses among Costa Rican samples. Panel A 610 shows the overall relationship between genetic and geographic distance across the whole 611 country, with an observed Mantel correlation of 0.05 (p = 0.11), indicating no significant IBD at 612 the country scale. Panels B, C, and D show IBD within the regions with extensive sampling, 613 ordered from north to south. Observed correlations and resulting p values also demonstrate a lack 614 of significant isolation by distance at the regional scale. 615

Figure 9. Discriminant Analysis of Principal Components (DAPC) of Costa Ricana cacao 640 genotypes, showing genetic clustering of individuals independent of sampling location. Each 641 point represents an individual tree, with colors corresponding to unsupervised genetic clusters. 642 Cluster 1 (light green) corresponds to Amelonado materials and those with dominant Amelonado 643 ancestry, while Cluster 3 corresponds to Criollo materials. Cluster 2 (dark grey) reflects 644 individuals with mixed ancestry. Axes represent the first (DA1) and second (DA2) discriminant 645 functions, explaining 68% and 32% of the between-cluster genetic variation, respectively. The 646 spatial distribution of points and clusters highlights the mosaic of genetic ancestries across Costa 647 Rican cacao collections, illustrating that while Criollo and Amelonado individuals are genetically 648 distinct, most trees collected exhibit extensive admixture. 649

Figure 3. Observed heterozygosity was calculated using genotype likelihoods in ANGSD. 384 Boxplots summarize the distribution of Hₒ within each farm (center line = median; box limits = 385 interquartile range, whiskers = 1.5 x IQR). Individual trees are overlaid as points to show within-386 farm variation. Point shape denotes region of origin: circles = Guatuso-Upala, squares = 387 Guácimo, triangles = Talamanca, and diamonds = Puntarenas. The number of trees sampled per 388 farm is indicated below each farm as labelled on the x-axis. 389

Figure 2. Kinship network graph depicting the genetic relationships of duplicate and closely-351 related Costa Rican individuals. Red lines connecting two collections indicate duplicate 352 accessions, identified by kinship coefficients between 0.354 and 0.5. The thicker the line, the 353 stronger the relationship. Orange lines indicate closely-related individuals, with first-degree 354 relatives (parent-offspring, full siblings) determined by a threshold between 0.25 and 0.354, 355 again with the thickness of the line indicating the strength of the relationship. The names of 356 Costa Rican collections have been enlarged for emphasis. Clusters of closely-related reference 357 accessions have been retained to demonstrate the general genetic similarity of investigated cacao 358 genetic material. 359
Figure 5. Principal component analysis (PCA) of Costa Rican cacao collections. 542

Figure 7. Phylogenetic network inferred using the NeighborNet algorithm. The network is 590 composed of 11 of the most admixed samples (MaxQ ≤ 0.4 and normalized entropy ≥ 0.65) 591 alongside two representatives per each of the nine genetic clusters present in Costa Rica (colored 592 according to their group). In the NeighborNet network, each tip represents an individual sample. 593
